entries.provenance: repair round assisted by reviewer defect findings; no external solutions
entries.justification: Re-grade after the repair round: both previously identified defects are correctly fixed and the fixes hold under exact re-verification. (1) Lemma 1.1's Xiang Yu direction now has the right turn parity - after LB takes p_j and XY takes the largest remaining piece, LB moves first on M'', so XY is the SECOND player there and by induction secures even(M''); the case identities (l + even(M'') - even(M) telescopes to (p_1-p_2)+(p_3-p_4)+... >= 0, with correct j-parity endings and the j=1 and empty-multiset base cases handled) all check, and exact minimax on 860 multisets (exhaustive small integer + random rational) confirms the game value is exactly odd(M)/even(M); on the reviewer's {3,2,1} the old claim (XY secures 3) is false and the new claim (XY secures 2 = even(M)) is tight. (2) Theorem 4.2 now asserts a subset B AND an ordering (e_1,...,e_r) with chain value <= S/(2^m-1); the proof constructs exactly that ordering via the greedy realizability Lemma 4.1, and XY's strategy explicitly processes 'e_2,...,e_r in this order'. I simulated the full pipeline (pigeonhole -> distinct subsets -> greedy ordering -> literal cutting strategy) in exact rationals on 600 compositions for n = 1..5: always <= n cuts, final D = chain value <= 1/(2^{n+1}-1), and I found compositions where the sorted-order chain value grossly violates the bound (e.g. 21469/14960 vs bound 198613/942480), so the fix was load-bearing, not cosmetic. Spot-checks found nothing broken by the repair: the matching formula (Lemmas 2.1-2.3), Part A's loop/isolated-vertex tree-component argument with the margin inequality, Lemma 4.1's three stuck-freeness cases, the cut-count/mark-distinctness/interiority accounting, and the mid-stream zero running-value case all verify.
key_issues: Marks at the endpoints 0/1 never discussed (zero-length pieces); harmless under the natural reading and shared by the other 7-scored solutions

entries.justification: Complete and rigorous proof of both directions, verified line by line and machine-tested. Lemma 2 (claiming-phase value = odd-rank sum) is proved with correct rank bookkeeping (2.1)/(2.2) for BOTH players and avoids the kimi-k3 turn-parity slip: after the opponent's first claim a_j the second player is first-to-move on A\{a_j} and O(A\{a_j}) >= E(A); I confirmed the exact minimax equals the odd-rank sum on 300 random rational multisets. Lower bound: the adjacent-rank pair multigraph on the n+1 geometric pieces has <= n edges, forcing a simple tree component; a proper 2-colouring turns the paired slacks into a signed sum of distinct powers of 2 with |Xi| >= u, so D >= u (isolated-vertex and unpaired-part cases check out). Upper bound: subset-sum pigeonhole gives disjoint A*, B* with 0 <= Delta <= u, and the concatenated-row cross-pairing marking (count (m-p-q)+q+(p-1) = n exactly bounded, all marks interior and distinct) makes both rows share the same grid Gamma on [0, beta_q], leaving only a leftover of mass Delta, so D <= u by the equal-pairs cancellation lemma. I re-implemented the Lemma 5 construction literally in exact rational arithmetic: on 2000 random configurations (m = 2..6) it always used <= n marks and achieved D <= u; an n=1 grid brute force reproduces c = 2/3.
key_issues: Marks at the endpoints 0/1 are never discussed (setup silently restricts to the open interval); harmless under the natural reading and shared by the other 7-scored solutions

entries.justification: Correct answer, correct global architecture (reduction to the alternating imbalance, the dyadic construction, and a two-part refinement lemma that is exactly the right statement), and a genuinely rigorous claiming-phase argument via matching greedy strategies. However, BOTH bounds rest entirely on the 'column sweep' proof of the refinement lemma, which is an unverifiable sketch even in the fuller lemmas/refinement.md: the upper-bound recursion's bookkeeping is asserted, not proved (why cancellation splits the remaining mass at most evenly between parities, why the 'sides' are valid sub-instances of the induction, and the initial odd segment of measure d appears to add to the recursive d rather than yielding a total of d), and the converse strip-preservation induction is a one-sentence assertion that ignores how cuts flip parity both below min and above max of the split. As submitted, neither direction is actually proved, so the 'solved' claim is a substantial overclaim.
key_issues: Refinement lemma part (i): sweep/cancellation induction not a valid proof as written - the recursed 'arrays' are not instances of the lemma, the (2^s-1)d half-split of T-d is unjustified, and the accounting seemingly yields d (initial) + d (recursive) rather than the claimed d

Refinement lemma part (ii): the strip-survival alternative ('either a whole d-strip remains or every column was cut') is asserted, not proved; a split flips parity on [0,min) and [max,a), contradicting the claim that cuts act 'only above their level'
Both the lower and upper bounds depend entirely on this unproven lemma

entries.justification: Complete and rigorous proof of both directions; the default run's unproven 'column sweep' lemma is entirely gone, replaced by the sound architecture. Section 1 proves the claiming value V = D via the minimax recurrence with the explicit cancellation identity (2), which I verified by hand for both parities and machine-tested against exact minimax on 200 random multisets. Section 2's exchange argument (min pairing cost = D, odd case via an appended 0) is correct, giving the key tool (4). Section 3's common-refinement cutting fact has exact cut bookkeeping ((E-s)+(E-r)+1 <= |A|+|B|-1 with E <= r+s-1, tail case included), which I re-derived and confirmed. Lower bound: powers-of-two intervals, adjacent-pair multigraph with <= n edges on n+1 vertices forces a tree component; the bipartition of (7) gives a nonzero signed sum of distinct powers of two, so D >= d - all steps check (loops handled via doubled degree, isolated vertex allowed). Upper bound: m <= n bisection case, subset-sum pigeonhole (gap <= d among 2^{n+1} sums), symmetric-difference reduction, and the refinement construction with total cuts exactly (|A|+|B|-1)+(n+1-|A|-|B|) = n. I re-implemented the XY strategy literally in exact rationals (400 random markings: always <= n cuts and D <= d) and grid-searched XY responses to the lower-bound construction for n = 1, 2 (min D = d exactly).
key_issues: Marks at the endpoints 0/1 never discussed (zero-length pieces); harmless under the natural reading and shared by the other 7-scored solutions

entries.justification: A stream-of-consciousness tracking file, honestly self-reported 'partial'. The conjectured answer (n+1)/(2n+1) is WRONG for all n>=2 (it coincides with the correct 2^n/(2^{n+1}-1) only at n=1, both = 2/3). Only the n=1 case is genuinely handled. The single substantive general claim - that Liu's 'even-multiples' construction (n pieces of size 2d and one of size d, d=1/(2n+1)) guarantees Liu (n+1)/(2n+1) - is FALSE, and is exactly the calibration's 'false XY-guarantee' failure mode. Its internal invariance sub-lemma (splitting each big piece once yields Liu-share (n+1)d, cancelling deltas by matched parity of positions i and 2n+2-i) is itself mathematically correct, but the fatal gap is assuming Xiang's best response is to split each big piece once while leaving the small piece 'untouched'. I machine-tested n=2 ({0.4,0.4,0.2}, Xiang 2 cuts): exact minimax (agreeing with the sum-of-odd-ranks formula) shows Xiang holds Liu to 101/200 = 0.505 by cutting the SMALL piece into slivers - far below the claimed 0.6 and even below the true optimum 4/7 ~ 0.571. There is no upper-bound argument at all; the file explicitly ends stuck ('need different Xiang strategy to upper bound', 'let's attempt to search computationally'). Correct n=1 plus a correct claiming-game value understanding keep it just above 0.
key_issues: Final answer (n+1)/(2n+1) is wrong for every n>=2
Central lower-bound claim is false: machine-tested n=2, Xiang forces Liu to 0.505 < 0.6 by slicing the small piece (a response never considered)
No upper bound whatsoever; author explicitly stuck
The (correct) invariance lemma only covers 'split each big piece once', not the full space of Xiang responses

entries.justification: The 'solved' claim does NOT hold up - this is a substantial overclaim, and the run was cut off by the 80-turn cap (the file literally ends with python string-literal artifacts '""")' and 'print("done writing")', i.e. it was mid-write). Genuine correct content: the correct answer; the alternating-sum functional D with V(L)=(sum+D)/2; and Lemma A (subadditivity D(X u Y)<=D(X)+D(Y) and reverse-triangle D(X u Y)>=|D(X)-D(Y)|), which IS a real non-trivial lemma correctly proved via the integral/parity representation (I verified delta_{XuY}=delta_X XOR delta_Y gives both inequalities). The power-of-two construction and the reduction (lower bound <=> Theorem B: D>=1 after <=n cuts on B_n={1,2,4,...,2^n}) are the right architecture. BUT neither bound is actually proved. Theorem B's induction: the k=0 base is fine, but the k>=1 step is garbled and hand-waved - it contains literal placeholders like '(something that forces)', multilingual gibberish, and openly substitutes 'numerical checks for n<=3' and 'checked exhaustively by hand' for the general argument. Theorem C (upper bound) is worse: multiple false starts, an admitted contradiction ('This appears contradictory unless...'), a 'the correct logic is dual' restart, and a hand-waved 'binary Greedy Egyptian fraction algorithm' whose '<=n cuts' cost is asserted, never proved. This maps almost exactly onto gpt-5.6-sol (score 2): correct answer + correct global architecture + a genuinely rigorous claiming-phase component, but BOTH bounds rest on unproved/garbled sketches, so neither direction is established.
key_issues: Both bounds unproved: Theorem B's k>=1 induction step is garbled hand-waving; Theorem C (upper bound) never completes and contains an admitted contradiction
Run hit the 80-turn cap; output is corrupted (multilingual gibberish tokens) and cut off mid-write (trailing '""")'/'print("done writing")')
Substitutes 'numerical checks for n<=3' and 'checked by hand' for the general inductive proof in both directions
Salvageable correct pieces (answer, D-functional, Lemma A proved via integral, construction) do not amount to a proof of either bound

entries.justification: An honest 'partial' tracking file that is essentially a conjecture with no proof. The stated answer (n+1)/(2n+1) is WRONG for every n>=2 (it equals the correct 2^n/(2^{n+1}-1) only at n=1, both 2/3); only n=1 is genuinely right. The lower-bound claim - that Liu's equally-spaced marks {1/(2n+1),...,n/(2n+1)} guarantee (n+1)/(2n+1) because 'Xiang's optimal response makes all 2n+1 pieces equal' - is exactly the calibration's flagged false-XY-guarantee. I verified in exact arithmetic (claiming value = odd-rank sum, confirmed on 200 multisets and on {3,2,1}->4): against DeepSeek's OWN n=2 construction {0.2,0.2,0.6}, Xiang holds Liu to 53/105 ~ 0.5048 by slicing the small piece, far below the claimed 0.6 and below the true optimum 4/7 ~ 0.5714 (which a powers-of-two construction {1/7,2/7,4/7} achieves, verified). Even the file's brute-force data is wrong (it reports n=2 = 3/5 = 0.6; the true value is 4/7). No upper bound is attempted ('Full proof: (To be completed)'), no rigorous lemma is present, and the claiming subgame is treated naively (first player just takes n+1 of 2n+1 equal pieces) with none of the odd-rank-sum insight. Strictly weaker than the calibrated score-1 muse-spark entry (same wrong answer, but that one also had a correct invariance lemma and correct claiming-value understanding); kept just above 0 only by the correct n=1 value, the honest self-report, and correct two-phase framing.
key_issues: Final answer (n+1)/(2n+1) is wrong for every n>=2 (correct: 2^n/(2^{n+1}-1))
Central lower-bound claim is the flagged false-XY-guarantee: verified Xiang holds Liu to 53/105 ~ 0.505 < 0.6 against DeepSeek's own {0.2,0.2,0.6} construction for n=2
No upper bound and no proof of either direction; 'Full proof: (To be completed)'
Brute-force data itself is wrong (claims n=2 = 3/5; true value is 4/7)
Claiming subgame handled naively; no odd-rank-sum insight, no invariance lemma
